titleStmt.title: Introduction to the site of Mount Ge
titleStmt.author: Claudia

figure.head: 由西南方遠眺葛山山峯；2008年拍攝
Peak of Mount Ge seen from the southwest; photograph taken in 2008

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 葛山由一組南北方向延伸0.4公里的小山丘組成，最北側有一獨特的山峰（）。而其東西方向延伸為0.7公里，山頂海拔高230米，山腳海拔高170米。葛山位於鄒城以東14公里、匡莊村東北4公里處。
葛山位於東經117.1084度，北緯35.4294度。相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行509840，北行3920670。西麓是葛山最大的一片連貫岩面（），其坡度基本保持在30至35%度之間。
銘文刊刻在西麓的最低處，面向西南偏西。文字隨著石坡的高低起伏而刻。1972年圍繞部分刻經面築起了保護石牆，附近還建造了一個現代小石屋（）。在刻經表面上方，山體至山脊的頂端提升約100米。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 緊鄰的山峰有在其西南13公里處的嶧山、西北偏西8.2公里處的尖山。在葛山的西麓可以眺望這兩座山的山頂，不過無法看見這兩座山上的刻經。
由於葛山刻經位於一片獨立的坡岩上，因此可以像在鐵山和泰山經石峪那樣，踏上這片刻經面。不過鐵山刻經的整體結構為一方岩面上的巨型石碑，而葛山的刻經除了呈長方形外，並沒有碑額或底座的任何痕跡。
葛山銘刻的佛經節文來自鳩摩羅什所翻譯的《維摩詰所說經》，《維摩詰所說經》T#475, 14: 554c28－555a24。共十行，每行40字。只有第一行和最後一行可能有41字。但是因為風化，第一行的刻字總數不能確定。經與傳世文本比較，佛經節文的389字當時應已全數刊刻上石。
此外，在佛經節文第三行至第五行的正下方，有一則題記。

div.head: 釋名
div.p: 葛山，又名葛婁山或木可婁山。 葛婁山之名或來自葛婁莊，相傳著名道士葛洪（約280年－約340年）曾在此煉丹。金石學家段松苓（1745－1800）因此稱此處為葛家樓村。
吳若灝修於光緒年間（1875－1908）的《鄒縣續志》（光緒十八年［1892］作序）載：“城東二十五里，南葛婁山、北葛婁山二山相連。”《鄒縣續志》 第521頁。因知當地人稱此山為北山，並無葛山之謂。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 葛山附近文獻可考或有實物出土的寺院有二：一、 鄒城的法興寺。 二、今平陽寺村的前觀音寺，位於鄒城以西20公里處。

清康熙十二年刻本《鄒縣志》載，縣城北門有法興寺，基址二十畝。《鄒縣志》第194頁。根據陳夢家第38頁，清代一畝等於614.4平方米。此後，由於縣城擴大，寺院原址今歸入縣城之內。法興寺應是北朝後期，縣城由嶧山之前北遷後的“縣立”寺院，根據婁一均於清康熙五十四年（1715）所修《鄒縣志》所載，縣城遷移時間在南宋元嘉十三年（436）。見《鄒縣志》第281頁。而《嘉慶重修一統志》（1842年序）則記載，縣城遷移於北齊天保七年（556）。《嘉慶重修一統志》第166卷；第59冊：第3a頁。元代重修時改名重興寺，寺址存有宋代的磚塔。
《鄒縣志》還給出了觀音寺的位置並涉及其1637年的重建。《鄒縣志》第195頁。1975年，四尊北魏、東魏和北齊的雕像在現今的平陽寺村出土，可能是俗家弟子供奉給寺院的。
上述兩座寺院從北朝起就香火不斷，卻未發現任何與葛山刻經的直接關聯。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 由於葛山唯一僅存的題記（葛山2號）風化嚴重，也並無他處記有刻經人事蹟，因此與刻經直接相關的人物今已不可考。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 刻經的最後一行，空格之後有一條13字的紀年，“大像兩秊歳玄枵律俠鐘廿六日”，對應的西曆時間為580年3月27日。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 最早記錄葛山刻經的學者是黃易（號小松，1744－1802），隨後是阮元（1764－1849）和段松苓。他們已知刻經的年款，只不過僅依據拓本片斷，所以沒有考慮到銘文及其內容的整體布局。金石學家孫星衍（1753－1818）、尹彭壽（1835－1904－）和法偉堂（1843－1907）都沿用了阮元“摩崖兩種”的錯誤分類。直到吳式芬（1796－1856）在《金石彙目分編》中才意識到刻經節文由十行組成。
直至李佐賢（1807－1876）親自考察葛山，才最終糾正了阮元對刻經的錯誤釋讀。他在《石泉書屋金石題跋》中，還記錄了位於刻經第五行之下的題記（葛山2號）。
孫葆田（1840－1911）將每行刻經都謄錄在其《山東金石志》中。但因所依據拓本尚缺部分經文，所以他的錄文仍不完整。他也惋惜銘文惡劣的保存狀態。

1990年，王鈞和阿濤發表了首張葛山刻經的經文布局復原圖，並辨識了刻經節文。在20世紀90年代及21世紀初，山東省石刻藝術博物館出版的重要刊物和書籍也討論了葛山刻經。其中，在1991年出版的《北朝摩崖刻經研究》中，胡新立作了一份葛山刻經的綜述報告，包括尺寸和字數，並將該處的刻經與鐵山刻經作了比較。他還提到經文下的題記，並引用了李佐賢的紀錄。另外，清原実門除了辨識出經文之外，同時也評估了清代的金石學紀錄。他推測僧安道壹（活動於562－579年）可能為葛山刻經的供養人，雖然其名並未出現在風化嚴重的題記之中。桐谷征一則提出了山東省境內所有佛教摩崖刻經均與清原実門所提的安道壹，即僧安道壹有關，並將葛山的刻經與鐵山及崗山刻經一起歸為僧安道壹的最晚期作品。
1997年，安廷山出版了一部作品涉及山東省內所有的摩崖銘刻遺址。該書倚重於討論各遺址的書法，其中包括了一份葛山銘刻書法風格的分析。
2000年，賴非又提供了一份當時最完善的錄文和單個刻字保存狀態的紀錄。

figure.head: 葛山西麓及刻經；2008年拍攝
Western slope of Mount Ge with sutra carving; photograph taken in 2008

div.head: 銘文總數
div.p: 葛山共刊刻一篇佛經節文（葛山1號）和一則歷史題記（葛山2號）。
葛山所刻經文節選自鳩摩羅什（344－413）翻譯的《維摩詰所說經》《維摩詰所說經》T#475, 14: 554c28－555a24。第十二品“見阿閦佛品”。該節文與山東數處刻經一樣，都是與“觀佛”有關。葛山選刻的部分可視為整部佛經的高潮，因為其內容敘述了維摩詰和釋迦牟尼佛唯一一次面對面的相遇。在第十一品中，維摩詰終於決定離開他與文殊菩薩展開辯論的居家，到佛陀說法的菴羅樹園去。
接下來這段釋迦摩尼佛和維摩詰之間的討論，呈現了這部佛經的主要教義之一，即正確的觀想如來。維摩詰認為自身或任何修行者身體的實相，都與如來身的實相無異（）。他並給出了一個充滿了悖論、極富技巧的描述，引起了很多中國經註者的爭論。
最早給鳩摩羅什所譯的《維摩詰所說經》作註的是其學生僧肇（374－414?），在他的《注維摩詰經》十卷《注維摩詰經》T#1775, 38: 327a10－419c7。中，還收錄了鳩摩羅什自己以及另一位學生道生（355－434）的經注。針對葛山選刻的佛經節文，僧肇解釋說：
div.quote: 實相之相，即如來相。故經曰：“見實相法為見佛也。” 淨名自觀身實相，以為觀如來相。義存於是。《注維摩詰經》T#1775, 38: 410a12－14。
div.p: 另外，自身實相等同於佛性。與葛山刻經同時期的註疏支持了這一觀點。三論師吉藏（549－623）的《淨名玄論》寫道：
div.quote: 觀身實相，觀佛亦然。故心、佛及眾生，是三無差別。道遠乎哉。
觀身實相，觀佛亦然。法身既即是身中實相。故知實相，佛性也。《淨名玄論》T#1780, 38: 903b4－10。
div.p: 據傳為吉藏所著的註疏《勝鬘寶窟》也表達了相同的看法：
div.quote: 維摩云：“觀身實相，觀佛亦然。” 是故眾生身即法身。《勝鬘寶窟》T#1744, 37: 67c15－16。
div.p: 關於葛山刻經節文還有更多的註疏，譬如智顗（538－597）關於《妙法蓮華經》的註疏《觀音玄義》，其門人灌頂（561－632）記載如下：
div.quote: 淨名云：“觀身實相，觀佛亦然。” 觀身相既等於佛觀心相，亦等於佛。《觀音玄義》T#1726, 34: 887b20－21。
div.p: 相較於《大正藏》本及其他現存的石刻版本，如原立於泗水縣天明寺的“大齊鄉老舉孝義雋修羅之碑”本和房山雲居寺刻經本，葛山刻經上的版本有一處異文。在其他版本中，世尊問維摩詰：“汝欲見如來，為以何等觀如來乎?” 只有葛山的版本在佛陀的問題中添了一個字，而讀作：“汝欲見如來，為以何等相觀如來乎?”
添加的這個字“相”將佛陀的問題更加清晰地指向整篇經文的中心議題，即如來實相與自身實相無異。隨後維摩詰的回答繼續圍繞“相”字：“如自觀身實相，觀佛亦然。”

事實上，這個答案是令人驚訝的。鳩摩羅什翻譯的《維摩詰所說經》是現存唯一一個包括自身和如來實相這一說法的譯本。傳世譯本中最早的支謙（活動於220－252年）本《佛說維摩詰經》T#474, 14: 534b17－c9。沒有任何相關闡述，且不包括維摩詰答案中這第一句話。其後以忠於梵文原文而聞名的玄奘（約602－664）譯本使用了與鳩摩羅什完全不同的翻譯方式。在這篇經文中，維摩詰說：“我觀如來，都無所見。如是而觀。”《說無垢稱經》T#476, 14: 584a16－b29。
本篇節文並沒有涉及專注於自身和相的觀想。玄奘的表述則強調觀如來不同於其他所有的視覺體驗，因為“都無所見”。視覺的空是這裡理解的核心。玄奘的翻譯因此與鳩摩羅什譯本中的“實相”相對。
目前所知，只有一個12世紀的梵文版《維摩詰所說經》傳世。大正大學綜合佛教研究所的高橋尚夫在1999年於拉薩布達拉宮發現該貝葉梵文寫本，見大正大學綜合佛教研究所第434－435頁。如今《大正藏》所集的三個漢譯本，只有玄奘譯本接近梵文本。不過，兩者之間仍有不同。玄奘翻譯為：“我觀如來都無所見……”，而此句的梵文應該理解為：“我以不見［如來］而觀如來……”。
明顯地，鳩摩羅什表述“觀”的內容與後來玄奘的翻譯以及梵文本都不同。維摩詰回答的第一句話甚至也可能是被添加到佛經原文之中的。相同的表述，“如自觀身實相，觀佛亦然”，也見於曼陀羅仙（於503年至中國）翻譯的《文殊師利所說摩訶般若波羅蜜經》。《文殊師利所說摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#232, 8: 728a26－29。
這段佛經節文也刊刻在山東其他的刻經山上，即洪頂山（洪頂山1號）和水牛山（水牛山1號）。山東摩崖的經主和刻經者們可能對觀自身和觀佛實相的命題有著特殊的興趣。

div.head: 葛山2號
div.p: 在刻經第三行至第五行之下的岩面，有小字題記的痕跡。由於岩面風化，僅個別刻字尚存。不過，可以確定此處曾經刊刻了經主的名字。依照常例，在經主名字前很可能刻有供養人籍貫和供養的內容。在這裡，至少三位供養人冠以“□心主”，原文很可能為“發心主”。若是如此，數名供養人則在此“發［菩提］心”願。山東其他刻經址常將供養人稱做“經主”或“像主”。很可能地，這篇《維摩詰所說經》節文被視為最佳“發心”文選。

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 從筆道橫斷面分析葛山刻經技法，可為两類：

list.item: “V”字形刻法。即從筆畫兩邊線斜入鏨，雙邊線相交，自然形成，方便快捷。適用於0.7至4釐米寬的筆畫鐫刻。葛山99%的刻經採用此技法。
“雙鈎”刻法。即將筆畫雙邊線單鏨刻出，留出筆畫內痕。適用於4釐米以上寬的筆畫。葛山極少數的字採用此刻法。

figure.head: 環繞葛山刻經面的石牆；2008年拍攝
Stone wall around the carved rock surface; photograph taken in 2008

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Ge 葛山 has a pronounced peak at the northern end of a low ridge running north-south for 0.4 km (fig. ). It has an east-west extension of 0.7 km. The peak is at an altitude of 230 m, and the foot is at 170 m above sea level. Mount Ge lies 14 km east of the city of Zoucheng 鄒城 and 4 km northeast of Kuang­zhuang Village 匡莊.

Geographically, Mount Ge is situated 117.1084° east and 35.4294° north, WGS84. This equals 509840 E (easting) and 3920670 N (northing), UTM zone 50S. The western slope, the largest coherent rocky surface on Mount Ge (fig. ), has a constant incline of 30–35%.

The inscriptions were chiseled into the lowest part of the western slope, facing west-southwest. Their characters follow the bumps and hollows of the rock incline. A protective stone wall was built around part of the inscribed surface in 1972, and a modern stone shelter has been constructed nearby (fig. ). Above the inscribed surface, the hill rises ca. 100 m to the top of the ridge.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p: The closest mountains are Mount Yi, 13 km to the southwest, and Mount Jian, 8.2 km to the west-northwest. While the peaks of both mountains can be seen from the western slope of Mount Ge, their inscriptions are not within the line of sight.

Since the inscriptions at Mount Ge have been placed on a single sloping rock, the site can be walked over, as at Mount Tie and at the Sutra Stone Valley on Mount Tai. While at Mount Tie, the sutra inscription is laid out as a colossal stele on the rock face, at Mount Ge, despite the inscription’s rectangular shape, a stele head and base are not indicated.

The main part of the inscription is a passage taken from the Sutra spoken by Vimalakīrti ( Vimala­kīrti­nirdeśa-sūtra , Weimojie suo shuo jing 維摩詰所說經 ) in Kumārajīva’s translation (T#475, 14: 554c28–555a24). It has ten vertical columns with 40 characters each. Only the first and last columns, probably, had 41 characters. However, because of erosion, the total number of characters in the first column cannot be determined. Looking at the received text it seems likely that, originally, 389 characters of the sutra were carved.
A colophon is placed directly below columns 3 to 5 of the sutra text.

div.head: Place Name
div.p: Mount Ge is also known as Mount Gelou 葛婁山 or Mount Mukelou 木可婁山. The name Mount Gelou may come from Gelou Village 葛婁莊: Tradition has it that the celebrated alchemist, Ge Hong 葛洪 (ca. 280–ca. 340), concocted pills of immortality here. The epigrapher Duan Songling 段松苓 (1745–1800) speaks about Gejialou Village 葛家樓村.

The Sequel to the Gazetteer of Zou County (Zou Xian xuzhi 鄒縣續志) from the Guangxu era 光緖 (1875–1908), which was revised by Wu Ruohao 吳若灏, notes that Mount Ge is “located 25 miles east of the city, where the two mountains of Southern and Northern Gelou are interconnected.” 521. The mountain is locally named Northern Mountain, Bei Shan 北山, and not simply Mount Ge.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: The known monasteries closest to Mount Ge are Faxing Monastery 法興寺 of Zoucheng, and the former Guanyin Monastery 觀音寺, which was located in today’s Pingyangsi Village 平陽寺村, about 20 km west of Zoucheng.

The Gazetteer of Zou County (Zou Xian zhi 鄒縣志), printed in 1673, reports that Faxing Monastery was at the Northern Gate of the county town, covering twenty mu 畝, 12.288 m². 194. According to 38, a mu in the Qing dynasty equals 614.4 m². Since that time, the county town has grown, so that the monastery is today within the area of the modern city. When, during the late Northern dynasties, the county seat was moved from its site below Mount Yi to the north,According to the Zou County Gazetteer (Zou Xian zhi 鄒縣志 ), revised by Lou Yijun 婁一均 in the fifty-fourth year of the Kangxi era (1715), the county seat was moved in 436, the thirteenth year of the yuanjia 元嘉 (424–453) era of the Southern Song dynasty. 281. The Unified Gazetteers [of the Qing Dynasty] Revised during the Jiaqing era (Jiaqing chongxiu yitong zhi 嘉慶重修一統志; preface 1842) states, however, that the county seat was moved in 556, the seventh year of the tianbao 天保 (550–559) era of the Northern Qi dynasty. juan 166; 59: 3a. the Faxing Monastery was designated the County Monastery. When restoration was undertaken in the Yuan dynasty, the monastery was renamed Chongxing Monastery 重興寺. On the site of the ruins, a brick pagoda of the Song dynasty still stands.

The Gazetteer of Zou County also gives the location of Guanyin Monastery and mentions a renovation of 1637. 195. In 1975, four sculptures of the Northern Wei, Eastern Wei, and Northern Qi dy­nas­ties were found in today’s Pingyangsi Village. They may have been donated by lay followers to the monastery.
Although these two monasteries florished without interruption from the time of the Northern dynasties, no direct link with Mount Ge’s rock cut inscriptions has been established.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: Since the only extant colophon at Mount Ge is heavily weathered, no individuals can be directly connected with the site.

div.head: Dating
div.p: A date in 13 characters in the last column of the inscription is separated by one empty space. It reads: the twenty-sixth day of the second month of the second year of the daxiang era, Jupiter in Aquarius 大像兩秊歳玄枵律俠鐘廿六日, corresponding to March 27, 580.

div.head: Research History
div.p: The first scholar who recorded the sutra inscription at Mount Ge was Huang Yi 黃易 (Huang Xiaosong 黃小松; 1744–1802). He was followed by Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849) and Duan Songling 段松苓. They knew of the date, but since they all relied on incomplete rubbings, they were not aware of the overall layout of the text or of its contents.

The epigraphers, Sun Xingyan 孫星衍 (1753–1818), Yin Pengshou 尹彭壽 (1835–1904–) and Fa Weitang 法偉堂 (1843–1907), all followed Ruan’s erroneous division into “two kinds of polished cliff inscriptions”. That the text actually consists of ten columns was recognized by Wu Shifen 吳式芬 (1796–1856) in his Categorized Edition of an Assembled Catalog of Epigraphica (Jinshi huimu fenbian 金石彙目分編).

As Li Zuoxian 李佐賢 (1807–1876) travelled to the site himself, he was able to correct some mistakes in Ruan Yuan’s readings. In his Colophons to Epigraphica from the Stone Well Studio (Shiquan shuwu jinshi tiba 石泉書屋金石題跋), he also noted the colophon (Ge 2) below column 5 of the sutra inscription.

Sun Baotian 孫葆田 (1840–1911) transcribed all the columns of the sutra inscription in his Epigraphic Records of Shandong (Shandong jinshi zhi 山東金石志). However, his transcripts remained incomplete, as he had to rely on rubbings that omitted part of the text. As with many other inscriptions that he saw, he also laments the poor state of this inscription.

In 1990, Wang Jun 王鈞 and A Tao 阿濤 offered a first reconstruction of the layout of the characters on Mount Ge and identified the carved sutra passage. Mount Ge was also included in the major studies published by the Shandong Stone Carving Art Museum in the 1990s and 2000s. In his publication of 1991, Hu Xinli 胡新立 gives a summary report on the site, with measurements and numbers of characters, and he compares the inscription on Mount Ge with that of Mount Tie. He also mentions the colophon below the sutra text and quotes Li Zuoxian’s account. Kiyohara Sanekado 清原実門 identifies the sutra and also evaluates the Qing dynasty epigraphic sources. He proposes that Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562–579) may have been a donor of this inscription, even though his name does not appear in the badly damaged colophon. Kiriya Seiichi 桐谷征一 puts forward the view that all rock inscriptions in Shandong Province were connected to the monk Seng’an Daoyi, whom he, like Kiyohara before him, calls An Daoyi. He places the inscription on Mount Ge in the last phase of the monk’s work, together with the inscriptions on Mount Tie and Mount Gang.
In 1997, An Tingshan 安廷山 published all rock inscription sites in Shandong Province, with particular emphasis on the calligraphy, including the stylistic analysis of that on Mount Ge.
In 2000, Lai Fei 賴非 established the best transcription to date and documented the state of preservation of each single character.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: At Mount Ge, one sutra passage (Ge 1) and one historical inscription (Ge 2) were carved.

div.head: Ge 1
div.p: The sutra passage is drawn from the twelfth chapter, entitled “Vision of Akṣobhya Buddha” (Jian Achu fo pin 見阿閦佛品), in Kumārajīva’s (鳩摩羅什; 344–413) translation of the Sutra Spoken by Vimalakīrti ( Vimalakīrtinirdeśa-sūtra , Weimojie suo shuo jing 維摩詰所說經; T#475, 14: 554c28–555a24). Like several other Shandong inscriptions, the passage selected for carving deals with Buddha contemplation (guan fo 觀佛). It may be considered the climax of the entire sutra, since it represents the only direct face to face encounter between Vimalakīrti and Buddha Śākyamuni. In chapter 11, Vimalakīrti finally decides to move from his house, where the debate with Mañjuśrī (Wenshushili 文殊師利) has taken place, to the garden of Āmrapāli (Anluoshu yuan 菴羅樹園), where the Buddha is preaching. The ensuing discourse between Śākyamuni and Vimalakīrti centers on the question of correct contemplation of the body of the Thus Come One ( tathāgata , rulai 如來), one of the main doctrinal issues of the sutra. Vimalakīrti argues that the real attributes of his own body, or of any practitioner’s body, do not differ from the real attributes of the Thus Come One’s body (fig. ). Here he gives a rather technical description, full of paradoxes, which has given rise to much debate among Chinese commentators.

The earliest commentary on the Sutra Spoken by Vimalakīrti
is the Zhu weimojie jing 注維摩詰經 in ten scrolls (T#1775, 38: 327a10–419c7), by Kumā­ra­jīva’s disciple Sengzhao 僧肇 (374–414?), which also contains remarks by Kumārajīva himself, as well as those by another disciple, Daosheng 道生 (355–434). In commenting upon the passage that is carved on Mount Ge, Sengzhao expounds:
div.quote: The attribute of the real attributes is the attribute of the Thus Come One. Therefore the sutra says: “To see the dharmas of the real attributes is to see the Buddha.” Vimalakīrti con­tem­plates the real attributes of his own body to contemplate the attributes of the Thus Come One. The meaning lies here.T#1775, 38: 410a12–14.

figure.head: 葛山西北面全景圖，圖示葛山1號、葛山2號位置，來源：谷歌地球™
Mount Ge seen from the northwest, with inscriptions Ge 1 and Ge 2, as provided by Google™Earth

div.p: Moreover, the real attributes of one’s own body were seen as tantamount to Buddha-nature. Commentaries contemporary with the carvings on Mount Ge support this view. The Profound Treatise on Vimalakīrti (Jingming xuanlun 淨名玄論) by Jizang 吉藏 (549–623), reads:
div.quote: Contemplating the real attributes of the body is just like contemplating the Buddha. Therefore, there is no difference between the three: the mind, the Buddha, and sentient beings. How far-reaching is the Way!
div.p: Further:
div.quote: Contemplating the real attributes of the body is just like contemplating the Buddha. Since the Truth Body ( dharmakāya ) is the real attribute in this very body, it is known that the real attribute is Buddha-nature.T#1780, 38: 903b4–10.
div.p: In another commentary, attributed to Jizang, the Gem Vault of the Śrīmālā-sūtra (Shengman baoku 勝鬘寶窟), the same idea is found:
div.quote: Vimalakīrti says: Contemplating the real attributes of the body is just like contemplating the Buddha. For that reason, the body of the sentient beings is the Truth Body ( dharmakāya ).T#1744, 37: 67c15–16.
div.p: Others also refer to the passage that was selected for Mount Ge. For example, Zhiyi’s (智顗, 538–597) Profound Meaning of the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanyin xuanyi 觀音玄義), which is his commentary on chapter 25 of the Lotus Sutra , recorded by his disciple Guanding 灌頂 (561–632), says:
div.quote: Vimalakīrti says: Contemplating the real attributes of the body is just like contemplating the Buddha. One contemplates that the attributes of one’s own body are equal to the attributes of the Buddha’s [body], that the attributes of one’s own mind are equal to those of the Buddha’s [mind].T#1726, 34: 887b20–21.
div.p: The text that was carved at Mount Ge differs in one important character, “attributes” (xiang 相), not only from the canonical versions in the Taishō edition, but also from all other surviving versions carved in stone: The Stele of the Great Qi Dynasty about Juan Xiuluo Recommended as Filial and Righteous by the Community Elders (Da Qi xiang­lao ju xiaoyi Juan Xiuluo zhi bei 大齊鄉老舉孝義雋修羅之碑), which was originally in Tianming Monastery 天明寺, in Sishui County 泗水縣; and the carved versions preserved at Cloud Dwelling Monastery 雲居寺, in Fangshan 房山. In all these versions, the World-Honored One asks Vimalakīrti: “When you wish to see the Thus Come One, based upon what do you contemplate the Thus Come One?” Only the Mount Ge version adds the one character to the Buddha’s question, resulting in the reading: “When you wish to see the Thus Come One, based upon which attributes do you contemplate the Thus Come One?”At Mount Ge the following is carved: “爾時世尊問維摩詰：汝欲見如來，為以何等相觀如來乎？”

This one additional character, “attributes,” relates the Buddha’s question more clearly to the central topic of the whole passage, namely the real attributes of the Thus Come One, which are no different from the body’s real attributes. It takes up the character xiang 相, “attributes,” from the following answer given by Vimalakīrti, which reads: “Just as I contemplate the real attributes of my own body, so also do I contemplate the Buddha.”

In fact, this answer is surprising. Kumārajīva’s translation is the only one that includes this statement about the body’s and the Thus Come One’s real attributes. The earliest extant translation by Zhiqian 支謙 (fl. 220–252)T#474, 14: 534b17–c9. shows no traces of any related formulation and does not include the entire first sentence of Vimalakīrti’s answer. The later translation by Xuanzang 玄奘 (ca. 602–664), who is renowned for his faithfulness to the Sanskrit originals, also has a formulation quite different from Kumārajīva’s rendering. In this passage, Vimalakīrti says: “When I contemplate the Thus Come One, there is nothing to see at all. In this way do [I] contemplate.”T#476, 14: 584a16–b29.

No reference to contemplation focused on the body and its attributes is found in this passage either. Xuanzang’s formulation emphasizes that contemplation of the Thus Come One differs from all other visual experience, since “there is nothing to see.” The absence of the visual is essential to “contemplation” as understood here. Xuanzang’s rendering thus corresponds to what is meant by “the real attribute” (shixiang 實相) in Kumārajīva’s translation.

Only one Sanskrit manuscript of the
Vimalakīrti-nirdeśa-sūtra
is known; it dates to the twelfth century.This palm-leaf Sanskrit manuscript was discovered in 1999 at the Potala Palace at Lhasa by Takahashi Hisao 高橋尚夫 of the Institute for Comprehensive Studies of Buddhism at Taishō University, see 434–435. Among the three extant Chinese translations in the Taishō Collection, only the translation by Xuanzang comes close to the Sanskrit version. Yet, there are some differences. While Xuanzang translates: “When I contemplate the Thus Come One, there is nothing to see at all…”, the Sanskrit text must be understood as “I contemplate the Thus Come One by not seeing [the Thus Come One]…”.

It is clear that the concept of contemplation in Kumārajīva’s version differs from that in Xuanzang’s later translation and that in the extant Sanskrit text. The first sentence of Vimalakīrti’s answer might even have been interpolated into the original passage. Another example of the same, evidently popular formulation – “just as I contemplate the real attributes of my own body, so also do I contemplate the Buddha” – is also found in a passage of the Chinese translation of the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī
( Sapta­śatikā­prajñāpāramitā-sūtra , Wenshushili suo shuo mohe bore boluomi jing 文殊師利所說摩訶般若波羅蜜經) by *Mandra[sena] (Mantuoluoxian 曼陀羅仙), who arrived in China in 503.T#232, 8: 728a26–29.

The passage was also carved elsewhere in Shandong Province, at Mount Hongding (HDS 1) and at Mount Shuiniu (SNS 1). The topic of contemplation of the real attributes of the body and of the Thus Come One may have been of particular interest for donors and creators of the Shandong polished cliff inscriptions.

figure.head: 葛山1號局部，第一行第30至32字，“身實相”刻文；2005年拍攝
Ge 1, detail, column 1, characters 30–32, reading “the true attributes of the body”; photograph taken in 2005

div.head: Ge 2
div.p:
Below columns 3 to 5 of the sutra text, traces of a colophon in small characters were found on the rock. Due to the overall weathered condition, only a few characters are still legible, but it can be safely assumed that originally the names of donors were given here. As usual they were probably preceded by the name of the place from which they came and an indication of the nature of the sponsor’s particular donation. In this case, at least three of the donors were named “[…] mind donor” □心主, originally probably “donor putting forth the [bodhi] mind” (faxin zhu 發心主). In this case, it would appear that several donors took a vow to “put forth the [bodhi] mind”. Other inscription sites in Shandong often characterize the donors as “donor of the sutra” (jingzhu 經主) or “donor of the image” (xiangzhu 像主). It is likely that the passage from the Sutra Spoken by Vimalakīrti
was chosen, because it was thought to be appropriate for “putting forth the bodhi mind.”

div.head: Carving techniques
div.p: The carving is mainly of the V-shaped kind; some isolated characters were done in double outline carving.

figure.head: 葛山1號局部，第四行第33字，“滅”刻字；2008年拍攝
Ge 1, detail, column 4, character 33, reading “extinct”; photograph taken in 2008
